Avangard Innovative

The Opportunity

22 Plants. Two Continents.

Is the Commercial Side as Connected as the Operations?
Avangard diverts hundreds of millions of pounds of plastic from landfills every year. That is a serious operational machine. The question is whether the commercial layer behind it can keep pace.

A two-sided business model -- sourcing materials from industrial partners on one end, selling processed materials to manufacturers on the other -- creates complexity that compounds as you scale.

Pricing different grades of material. Forecasting plant capacity against inbound supply. Managing relationships across sourcing partners, direct buyers, and branded product customers simultaneously.

The companies that win at this scale are the ones whose commercial teams have a single, connected view of all of it.

What We Noticed
01

A Two-Sided Model Needs Two-Sided Visibility

Avangard operates on both sides of the materials market -- acquiring from industrial partners and selling to manufacturers. Those two relationships require different commercial motions.

Without a unified view, sourcing agreements and sales agreements live in separate systems. That makes forecasting harder and leaves margin on the table when supply and demand signals do not connect.

02

Material Pricing Is Complex by Design

Not all recycled plastic is the same. Grade, source, processing level, and volume all affect price. Managing that complexity manually -- or in spreadsheets -- introduces inconsistency and slows the quoting process.

When pricing logic is systematized, the commercial team can quote accurately and quickly without relying on institutional knowledge that walks out the door.

03

A Branded Product Line Changes the Commercial Conversation

The Natura PCR line signals a move beyond commodity material sales toward a branded, customer-relationship-driven business. That requires a different kind of engagement -- more consultative, more sustained, more data-informed.

Building that engagement capability systematically is what separates a branded line that scales from one that stays small.

The Point of View

Operational Scale Without Commercial Infrastructure
Is an Execution Risk.

Avangard has built something operationally impressive -- 22 plants processing hundreds of millions of pounds of material per year across two continents. But operational scale and commercial sophistication do not grow at the same rate by default.

The commercial infrastructure has to be built intentionally. That means run-rate agreement tracking that gives leadership a reliable forecast on both sourcing and sales. It means pricing logic that is consistent across grades, volumes, and customer tiers. And it means a unified data layer that connects plant output, sourcing capacity, and customer demand into a single picture.

With a recent acquisition in the picture, the pressure to demonstrate commercial predictability to new ownership is higher than ever. That is exactly when this kind of infrastructure pays off.

What This Could Enable

Run-Rate Agreement Tracking Across Sourcing and Sales

Recurring supply agreements on the sourcing side and recurring sales agreements on the buyer side behave like subscription revenue -- predictable, but only if they are tracked that way.

When both are managed in a single system, leadership can see the full run-rate picture: what is coming in, what is going out, and where the gaps are before they become problems.

Consistent, Systematized Pricing Across Material Grades and Volumes

When material pricing is encoded in a system rather than carried in individual reps' heads, the team can quote faster, price more consistently, and adapt to market shifts without retraining the whole team.

It also gives leadership visibility into margin by grade and customer -- which products and relationships are actually driving profitability.

A Unified View Connecting Plant Output, Supply, and Customer Demand

Twenty-two plants across multiple countries generate a lot of operational data. The value of that data depends on whether it is connected to the commercial layer.

When plant output, inbound material volumes, and customer order patterns share a common data layer, the commercial team can make better decisions about pricing, allocation, and where to grow.

The Next Step

Worth 20 Minutes to Pressure Test

This is based on outside research, so some of the assumptions here may not reflect how the business works today.

How does the team forecast sourcing supply and material sales together today? Where does pricing live -- and who owns it when it changes? What does the Natura PCR commercial motion look like right now?

If any of this resonates, a short conversation is the right next step.
